# Hob: A Language for Hobson

## Implementation Plan

---

## Vision

Hob is a small, interpreted Lisp dialect designed specifically for Hobson. It combines Lisp's implementation model — s-expressions, an interpreter, macros, homoiconicity — with Smalltalk's interaction philosophy: live inspection, graphical tools, and the principle that the tool and the material are made of the same stuff.

The language has three distinguishing properties:

1. **Item references are a primitive type.** Reading an item is a language-level operation, not an API call. The runtime tracks dependencies automatically.
2. **Code is stored as items.** The AST is serialized into the item store, making code queryable, navigable, and modifiable with the same tools used for data.
3. **Async is invisible.** The interpreter's eval loop is async JavaScript internally, but Hob code is written and experienced as synchronous. The concept of promises does not exist in the language.

The end state is a Hobson where the kernel, all views, all libraries, and all user code are written in Hob, with the interpreter itself being the only JavaScript item. The development experience aspires to Smalltalk: a live browser where you navigate items, see their structure, click through references, modify code, and watch changes propagate instantly.

---

## Language Specification

### Primitive Types

| Type | Examples | Notes |
|------|----------|-------|
| Number | `42`, `3.14`, `-7` | IEEE 754 doubles |
| String | `"hello"`, `"line\nbreak"` | Immutable, standard escapes |
| Boolean | `true`, `false` | |
| Nil | `nil` | Absence of value |
| Keyword | `:title`, `:type`, `:on-click` | Self-evaluating, used as keys |
| Symbol | `foo`, `my-var`, `+` | Evaluated to their binding |
| Item Reference | `@my-note`, `@a1b2c3d4-...` | First-class reference to an item in the store |

Item references can use either a name (`@my-note`) or a UUID (`@a1b2c3d4-...`). The runtime resolves names to IDs at evaluation time. An unresolvable reference is an error.

### Compound Types

| Type | Syntax | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| List | `(1 2 3)` | Linked list; when evaluated, first element is called as function |
| Vector | `[1 2 3]` | Indexed array; evaluates to itself (no function call semantics) |
| Map | `{:name "Alice" :age 30}` | Key-value pairs; evaluates to itself |
| Set | `#{1 2 3}` | Unique values |

Vectors are used for Hiccup-style DOM descriptions: `[:div {:class "note"} "hello"]`.

Maps are used for item content, function options, and general data.

### Special Forms

These are handled directly by the evaluator, not as function calls.

**`def`** — Bind a name in the current module's scope.
```lisp
(def x 42)
(def greeting "hello")
```

**`fn`** — Create an anonymous function. Supports destructuring.
```lisp
(fn [x y] (+ x y))
(fn [{:keys [title body]}] (str title ": " body))
```

**`let`** — Local bindings. Supports destructuring. Independent bindings (those that don't reference earlier bindings in the same `let`) are evaluated concurrently — see Concurrency section.
```lisp
(let [x 10
      y (+ x 5)]
  (* x y))
```

**`if`** — Conditional. Two or three branches. Only `false` and `nil` are falsy.
```lisp
(if (> x 0) "positive" "non-positive")
```

**`do`** — Sequence of expressions; returns the last.
```lisp
(do
  (log "starting")
  (set-item! item)
  "done")
```

**`quote`** — Return the expression unevaluated.
```lisp
(quote (1 2 3))   ; => the list (1 2 3)
'(1 2 3)          ; shorthand
```

**`defmacro`** — Define a macro (see Macros section).

**`loop` / `recur`** — Tail-recursive loop.
```lisp
(loop [i 0 sum 0]
  (if (> i 10)
    sum
    (recur (+ i 1) (+ sum i))))
```

**`try` / `catch`** — Error handling.
```lisp
(try
  (get-item "nonexistent")
  (catch e
    (log (str "Error: " (:message e)))
    nil))
```

**`plet`** — Parallel let. All bindings are evaluated concurrently. Bindings must be independent (no binding may reference another binding in the same `plet`).
```lisp
(plet [notes (find-items {:type @note-type})
       tags  (find-items {:type @tag-type})]
  (merge-index notes tags))
```

### Item Access

**`.`** — Access a field on an item or map.
```lisp
(. @my-note :title)           ; => "Shopping List"
(. @my-note :type)            ; => @note-type (item reference)
(. @my-note :children)        ; => vector of child specs
(. some-map :key)             ; works on plain maps too
```

**`..`** — Follow references through multiple items.
```lisp
(.. @my-note :type :name)     ; fetch note, get its type (a ref), fetch that, get name
```

Both `.` and `..` register dependencies with the reactivity tracker when evaluated inside a reactive context (e.g., during view rendering).

### Mutation

Hob is mostly functional, but persistent items and atoms require explicit mutation.

**`set-item!`** — Save an item to the store. The `!` suffix is a convention for side effects. The storage backend is implicit — the interpreter knows which store to use.
```lisp
(set-item! (assoc item :content (assoc (:content item) :title "New Title")))
```

**`atom`** — Create a mutable reference cell.
```lisp
(def counter (atom 0))
```

**`deref`** — Read an atom's value. No shorthand — the `@` sigil is reserved exclusively for item references.
```lisp
(deref counter)    ; => 0
```

**`swap!`** — Update an atom by applying a function.
```lisp
(swap! counter inc)     ; counter is now 1
(swap! counter + 5)     ; counter is now 6
```

**`reset!`** — Set an atom to a new value.
```lisp
(reset! counter 0)
```

### Functions

```lisp
;; Named function
(def add (fn [a b] (+ a b)))

;; Shorthand
(defn add [a b] (+ a b))

;; Multi-arity
(defn greet
  ([name] (greet name "Hello"))
  ([name greeting] (str greeting ", " name "!")))

;; Variadic
(defn log-all [& args]
  (each args (fn [a] (log a))))

;; Anonymous shorthand (for simple cases)
#(+ %1 %2)       ; equivalent to (fn [a b] (+ a b))
```

### Macros

Macros receive unevaluated syntax and return transformed syntax. They run at expansion time, before evaluation.

```lisp
(defmacro when [test & body]
  `(if ~test (do ~@body) nil))

(defmacro unless [test & body]
  `(if ~test nil (do ~@body)))

;; Threading macro — pipes a value through forms
(defmacro -> [val & forms]
  (reduce (fn [acc form]
    (if (list? form)
      (concat [(first form) acc] (rest form))
      (list form acc)))
    val forms))

;; Usage:
(-> @my-note
    (. :content)
    (. :title)
    (str/upper-case))
;; Expands to: (str/upper-case (. (. @my-note :content) :title))
```

Quasiquote syntax:
- `` ` `` — quasiquote (template)
- `~` — unquote (insert evaluated value)
- `~@` — unquote-splicing (insert elements of a list)

### Modules

Each code item is a module. Modules export bindings explicitly.

```lisp
;; In a library item named "string-utils"
(defn capitalize [s]
  (str (str/upper-case (str/first s)) (str/rest s)))

(defn truncate [s max-len]
  (if (<= (count s) max-len)
    s
    (str (subs s 0 (- max-len 3)) "...")))

(export capitalize truncate)
```

```lisp
;; In another item
(require string-utils)
(string-utils/capitalize "hello")   ; => "Hello"

;; Or import specific bindings
(require [capitalize truncate] from string-utils)
(capitalize "hello")                ; => "Hello"
```

The `require` form loads a code item by name, evaluates it, and returns its exports.

### Cross-language modules

`require` and `export` work through the kernel's `api.require` module system regardless of what language the library or caller is written in. The dispatch is:

1. `api.require('some-lib')` resolves the item by name, checks the cache
2. If the item has `content.code` — evaluated as an ES module (blob URL + `import()`)
3. If the item has `content.hob` — evaluated via a Hob interpreter instance, exports collected

In both cases, the result is a plain JS object of exports, cached by item ID. This works because Hob values *are* JS values — functions defined with `defn` are JS functions, vectors are arrays, maps are objects. No marshaling is needed.

This means the language boundary is invisible to both sides:

```lisp
;; Hob view requiring a JS library — works
(require markdown-it)

;; Hob view requiring a Hob library — also works, same path
(require string-utils)

;; JS view requiring a Hob library — also works
;; const utils = await api.require('string-utils');
```

The library's interpreter instance is created, runs, produces exports, and is discarded. The exported functions (closures over their Hob environment) survive as cached JS values. This is exactly how JS modules work too — evaluate once, cache the exports, discard the source context.

### Concurrency

The eval loop is async JavaScript internally, so individual Hob expressions are naturally sequential. However, many Hobson operations (item fetches, queries) are I/O-bound, and running them sequentially is unnecessarily slow. Hob provides concurrency at two levels.

**Automatic `let` parallelism.** The evaluator analyzes `let` bindings for data dependencies. If a binding does not reference any name defined earlier in the same `let` block, it is independent and can be evaluated concurrently with other independent bindings. This is a compile-time analysis — the evaluator scans each binding's AST for references to earlier names.

```lisp
;; These two bindings are independent → evaluated concurrently
(let [notes (find-items {:type @note-type})
      tags  (find-items {:type @tag-type})]
  ...)

;; 'y' depends on 'x' → evaluated sequentially
(let [x (get-item id)
      y (. x :title)]
  ...)
```

**Explicit parallel primitives.** For cases where automatic analysis isn't sufficient.

```lisp
;; plet — all bindings evaluated concurrently (no dependency analysis, all must be independent)
(plet [a (get-item id1)
       b (get-item id2)
       c (get-item id3)]
  (process a b c))

;; pmap — like map but evaluates the function on all elements concurrently
(pmap get-item ids)
(pmap (fn [note] (. note :title)) notes)
```

Both `plet` and `pmap` use `Promise.all` internally. They are built-in forms, not macros.

**Future: CPS-based interpreter.** The async/await evaluator has a fundamental limitation: CPU-intensive Hob computations (large maps, complex view trees) block the JavaScript thread until the next I/O await point, freezing the UI. A continuation-passing style (CPS) evaluator would solve this by making every evaluation step suspendable. The interpreter could yield after N steps, check for pending user input, and resume — like React's fiber architecture. CPS also enables cooperative concurrency (interleaving multiple computations), cancellation, and time-budgeted evaluation. This is the right long-term architecture for a Smalltalk-inspired live environment, but adds roughly 2x implementation complexity compared to async/await. Defer until there's concrete evidence of UI responsiveness problems (likely Phase 4+).

### Hiccup DOM Syntax

Views return vectors describing DOM structure. The interpreter's runtime translates these to real DOM.

```lisp
[:div {:class "note-view" :style {:padding "16px"}}
  [:h2 title]
  [:p body]
  [:button {:on-click (fn [e] (save! item))} "Save"]]
```

Rules:
- First element is a keyword (tag name): `:div`, `:span`, `:h2`, `:input`
- Optional second element is a map (attributes)
- Remaining elements are children (strings, numbers, or nested vectors)
- CSS class shorthand: `:div.note-view.active` → `<div class="note-view active">`
- ID shorthand: `:div#main` → `<div id="main">`
- Event handlers are functions: `{:on-click handler-fn}`
- `nil` children are skipped (enables conditional rendering)
- Vectors inside children are flattened (enables `map`/`for` producing lists)

### Views

A view is a code item that exports a `render` function. The `def-view` macro handles the boilerplate.

```lisp
(def-view @note-type [item]
  (let [{:keys [title body tags]} (:content item)]
    [:div.note-view
      [:h2 (or title "Untitled")]
      [:div.note-body (markdown body)]
      (when (seq tags)
        [:div.note-tags
          (for [tag-ref tags]
            (let [tag (resolve tag-ref)]
              [:span.tag (. tag :name)]))])]))
```

`def-view` is a macro that expands to:
1. Creating a view item with the render function as its code
2. Creating a view-spec item that binds the view to the target type
3. Registering reactivity: `item`, `resolve` calls, etc. are tracked

`resolve` fetches an item reference and registers a reactive dependency. When any resolved item changes, the view re-renders.

### Watches

```lisp
(def-watch "note-indexer"
  {:event :item/updated :type-extends @note-type}
  [event]
  (let [{:keys [item previous]} event]
    (when (not= (.. item :content :title) (.. previous :content :title))
      (update-search-index! item))))
```

`def-watch` expands to creating a library item with `content.watches` metadata and the handler function in `content.code`, exactly as the current declarative watch system works. Hob macros generate the same item structures — they just make them easier to author.

### Queries

```lisp
;; Simple filter query
(find-items {:type-extends @note-type
             :where (fn [item] (contains? (. item :tags) @project-tag))})

;; Datalog-style (future, requires query engine)
(query
  [?item :type-extends @note-type]
  [?item :content.tags ?tag]
  [?tag :name "project"])
```

The initial implementation would use the simple filter form, which maps directly to `getAll()` + filter. The Datalog-style syntax is a future aspiration that would require building a query engine — likely Phase 7 or beyond.

### FFI (JavaScript Interop)

For calling JavaScript when needed:

```lisp
;; Call a method on a JS object
(.method js-object arg1 arg2)

;; Access a JS property
(.-property js-object)

;; Set a JS property
(set! (.-property js-object) value)

;; Create a JS object
(js-obj :key1 val1 :key2 val2)

;; Import a JavaScript library item
(require-js "markdown-lib")   ; loads as JS, returns JS module object
```

The FFI is intentionally minimal. Most Hob code should never need it — the standard library and DOM runtime handle the common cases.

---

## Interpreter Architecture

The interpreter is a single JavaScript library item. It exports a function that the bootloader (or module system) calls to evaluate Hob source code.

### Components

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 Hob Interpreter                  │
│              (JavaScript library item)           │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Reader   │  │ Expander  │  │  Evaluator   │  │
│  │          │──▶│ (macros)  │──▶│  (async)     │  │
│  │ text→AST │  │ AST→AST   │  │  AST→values  │  │
│  └──────────┘  └───────────┘  └──────┬───────┘  │
│                                      │           │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌──────┴───────┐  │
│  │ Printer  │  │ Std Lib   │  │  Environment │  │
│  │          │  │ (built-in │  │  (scoping)   │  │
│  │ values→  │  │  fns in   │  │              │  │
│  │  text    │  │  JS)      │  │              │  │
│  └──────────┘  └───────────┘  └──────────────┘  │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │            DOM Runtime                    │    │
│  │  Hiccup vectors → real DOM, event         │    │
│  │  handler wiring, mount/unmount            │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │         Render Instance Registry          │    │
│  │  Indexes instances by item/view/parent,  │    │
│  │  enables reactive re-rendering           │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Reader

Recursive descent parser. Input: string. Output: nested arrays/objects representing the AST.

Internal AST representation (JavaScript objects):

```javascript
// Number: { type: 'number', value: 42 }
// String: { type: 'string', value: "hello" }
// Keyword: { type: 'keyword', value: 'title' }
// Symbol: { type: 'symbol', value: 'foo' }
// Item ref: { type: 'item-ref', value: 'my-note' } or { type: 'item-ref', value: 'a1b2c3...' }
// List: { type: 'list', elements: [...] }
// Vector: { type: 'vector', elements: [...] }
// Map: { type: 'map', entries: [[key, val], ...] }
```

The reader handles:
- Whitespace and comma skipping (commas are whitespace)
- Comments: `; line comment`
- String escapes: `\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`
- The `@` reader macro for item references (unambiguous — `@` always means item ref)
- Quasiquote: `` ` ``, `~`, `~@`
- Anonymous function shorthand: `#(+ %1 %2)`

### Macro Expander

Walks the AST before evaluation. When it encounters a list whose head is a known macro name, it calls the macro function with the unevaluated arguments and replaces the form with the returned AST.

Expansion is recursive — the result is re-expanded until no macros remain.

Core macros defined in the standard library (in Hob):

```lisp
(defmacro defn [name & decls] ...)
(defmacro when [test & body] ...)
(defmacro when-let [binding & body] ...)
(defmacro cond [& clauses] ...)
(defmacro -> [x & forms] ...)
(defmacro ->> [x & forms] ...)
(defmacro for [bindings body] ...)
(defmacro def-view [type params & body] ...)
(defmacro def-watch [name filter params & body] ...)
```

### Evaluator

The core async eval loop. This is the heart of the interpreter.

```javascript
async function evaluate(ast, env) {
  switch (ast.type) {
    case 'number':
    case 'string':
    case 'keyword':
    case 'boolean':
    case 'nil':
      return ast.value;

    case 'symbol':
      return env.lookup(ast.value);

    case 'item-ref':
      return resolveItemRef(ast.value, env);

    case 'vector':
      return Promise.all(ast.elements.map(el => evaluate(el, env)));

    case 'map':
      // evaluate all keys and values
      ...

    case 'list':
      return evaluateList(ast, env);
  }
}

async function evaluateList(ast, env) {
  const head = ast.elements[0];

  // Special forms
  if (head.type === 'symbol') {
    switch (head.value) {
      case 'def': return evalDef(ast, env);
      case 'fn': return evalFn(ast, env);
      case 'let': return evalLet(ast, env);  // with auto-parallel optimization
      case 'plet': return evalPlet(ast, env);
      case 'if': return evalIf(ast, env);
      case 'do': return evalDo(ast, env);
      case 'quote': return ast.elements[1]; // return unevaluated
      case 'loop': return evalLoop(ast, env);
      case 'try': return evalTry(ast, env);
      // ...
    }
  }

  // Function application
  const fn = await evaluate(head, env);
  const args = [];
  for (const arg of ast.elements.slice(1)) {
    args.push(await evaluate(arg, env));
  }

  const result = fn(...args);

  // Transparent async: if result is a Promise, await it
  if (result instanceof Promise) {
    return await result;
  }
  return result;
}
```

The `await` in the loop is what makes async invisible. Every function call that might touch IndexedDB or other async APIs goes through this loop, and promises are automatically awaited before the result is used.

#### Auto-Parallel `let`

`evalLet` analyzes bindings for data dependencies before evaluating:

```javascript
async function evalLet(ast, env) {
  const bindings = ast.elements[1].elements; // [name1, expr1, name2, expr2, ...]
  const body = ast.elements.slice(2);
  const childEnv = new Environment(env);

  // Build dependency graph: which bindings reference earlier ones?
  const pairs = [];
  const definedNames = new Set();
  for (let i = 0; i < bindings.length; i += 2) {
    const name = bindings[i].value;
    const expr = bindings[i + 1];
    const refs = freeSymbols(expr);  // static AST analysis
    const dependsOn = new Set([...refs].filter(r => definedNames.has(r)));
    pairs.push({ name, expr, dependsOn });
    definedNames.add(name);
  }

  // Group into batches: each batch's bindings are independent of each other
  // Batches are sequential (later batches may depend on earlier ones)
  const batches = topologicalBatch(pairs);

  for (const batch of batches) {
    if (batch.length === 1) {
      childEnv.define(batch[0].name, await evaluate(batch[0].expr, childEnv));
    } else {
      // Evaluate all bindings in this batch concurrently
      const results = await Promise.all(
        batch.map(b => evaluate(b.expr, childEnv))
      );
      batch.forEach((b, i) => childEnv.define(b.name, results[i]));
    }
  }

  return evalDo({ elements: body }, childEnv);
}
```

### Environment

Lexical scoping with closures. Each environment is a frame with a pointer to its parent.

```javascript
class Environment {
  constructor(parent = null) {
    this.bindings = new Map();
    this.parent = parent;
  }

  lookup(name) {
    if (this.bindings.has(name)) return this.bindings.get(name);
    if (this.parent) return this.parent.lookup(name);
    throw new Error(`Undefined: ${name}`);
  }

  define(name, value) {
    this.bindings.set(name, value);
  }

  extend(bindings) {
    const child = new Environment(this);
    for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(bindings)) {
      child.define(k, v);
    }
    return child;
  }
}
```

### Standard Library (Built-in Functions)

Implemented in JavaScript, registered in the global environment.

**Arithmetic**: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `mod`, `inc`, `dec`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `=`, `not=`

**Logic**: `and`, `or`, `not` (note: `and`/`or` are macros for short-circuiting)

**Strings**: `str` (concatenation), `subs`, `str/upper-case`, `str/lower-case`, `str/trim`, `str/split`, `str/join`, `str/includes?`, `str/replace`, `count` (overloaded for strings and collections)

**Collections**: `first`, `rest`, `cons`, `conj`, `concat`, `nth`, `get`, `assoc`, `dissoc`, `update`, `update-in`, `get-in`, `assoc-in`, `keys`, `vals`, `merge`, `into`, `count`, `empty?`, `seq`, `vec`, `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `some`, `every?`, `sort`, `sort-by`, `group-by`, `distinct`, `take`, `drop`, `partition`, `flatten`, `reverse`, `contains?`

**Concurrency**: `pmap` (parallel map)

**Item operations**: `get-item`, `set-item!`, `delete-item!`, `get-all`, `find-items`, `resolve`, `type-of`, `type-extends?`, `type-chain`

**Atoms**: `atom`, `deref`, `swap!`, `reset!`

**I/O and effects**: `log`, `log/warn`, `log/error`, `emit!` (emit an event)

**DOM**: Handled by the DOM runtime, not as explicit functions. The Hiccup vectors are the interface.

**Predicates**: `nil?`, `number?`, `string?`, `keyword?`, `symbol?`, `list?`, `vector?`, `map?`, `set?`, `fn?`, `item-ref?`, `atom?`

**Interop**: `js-obj`, `js-get`, `js-set!`, `js-call`, `js-new`

### DOM Runtime

Translates Hiccup vectors into real DOM elements. Handles mounting, event handler wiring, and cleanup.

**Mount**: Takes a Hiccup vector and a DOM container. Creates real DOM elements and inserts them.

**Re-render**: When a view re-renders (due to reactive dependency change), the runtime does a full replace of the view's DOM subtree. This is the simplest correct approach and avoids the complexity of building a custom virtual DOM diffing algorithm. A future optimization is to integrate a lightweight DOM diffing library (e.g., morphdom) for surgical patches — but this is deferred until benchmarks show it's needed.

**Event handlers**: When a Hiccup map contains `:on-click`, `:on-change`, etc., the runtime attaches real DOM event listeners that invoke the Hob function. Since Hob functions are closures managed by the interpreter, the runtime wraps them:

```javascript
function wrapHobHandler(hobFn, interpreter) {
  return function(domEvent) {
    // Convert DOM event to Hob-friendly map
    const hobEvent = {
      type: domEvent.type,
      target: domEvent.target,
      value: domEvent.target?.value,
      // ... other relevant fields
    };
    // Invoke the Hob function via the interpreter
    interpreter.apply(hobFn, [hobEvent]);
  };
}
```

**Component lifecycle**: When a DOM subtree is removed (parent re-render replaces it), the runtime cleans up event listeners and removes the instance from the render registry.

### Render Instance Registry

Enables reactive re-rendering for views.

The `RenderInstanceRegistry` indexes every rendered instance by `itemId`, `viewId`, and `parentId`. When an `item:updated` event fires, the registry looks up all instances displaying that item and schedules batched re-renders.

**Selector-filtered reactivity** (Hob views only): `(get-item id :select fn)` declares which slice of data the view cares about. The selector output is compared via `deepEquals` on each item change — re-renders fire only when the selected data actually changes.

**JS views**: No selector support — always re-render on own-item change.

See [Reactive Pipeline](item://c0c0c0c0-0082-0000-0000-000000000000) for full details.

---

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Interpreter

**Goal**: A working Lisp in the Hobson REPL. Can evaluate expressions, define functions, manipulate data.

**Deliverables**:
- Reader (text → AST)
- Evaluator (AST → values, with async transparency)
- Printer (values → text)
- Environment (lexical scoping, closures)
- Core standard library (arithmetic, strings, collections, predicates)
- Item operations (`get-item`, `set-item!`, `get-all`, `find-items`)
- Error messages with source location (line, column) — do not defer this
- REPL integration: a way to type Hob into the existing REPL and see results

**Implementation approach**: Single JavaScript library item (`hob-interpreter`). The REPL detects Hob code (e.g., by a prefix command like `:hob` or by detecting parentheses) and routes to the interpreter instead of JavaScript eval.

**Not included yet**: Macros, DOM, reactivity, views, concurrency. Those build on this foundation.

**Estimated size**: ~1500–2000 lines JavaScript.

**Testable milestone**: Open the REPL and run:
```lisp
:hob (let [notes (filter (fn [i] (= (:type i) "...note-type-id...")) (get-all))]
       (map (fn [n] (get-in n [:content :title])) notes))
;; => ("Shopping List" "Meeting Notes" "Project Ideas")
```

**Testing**

Phase 1 tests use the existing test-suite infrastructure (`test-lib`, `test-runner-service`, `test-runner-view`). The interpreter is loaded via `api.require('hob-interpreter')`, giving tests direct access to `read`, `evaluate`, `print`, and `createEnvironment`. Four test suites cover the four components:

**`hob-reader-tests`** (~25 tests)

Tests the reader (text → AST) in isolation. No evaluator needed.

- Primitives: numbers (`42`, `3.14`, `-7`), strings (`"hello"`, `"line\nbreak"`, `""`), booleans (`true`, `false`), `nil`, keywords (`:title`, `:on-click`), symbols (`foo`, `+`, `my-var`)
- Item references: by name (`@my-note`), by UUID (`@a1b2c3d4-...`)
- Collections: lists `(1 2 3)`, vectors `[1 2 3]`, maps `{:a 1 :b 2}`, sets `#{1 2 3}`, empty variants `()` `[]` `{}` `#{}`
- Nesting: `(def x [1 {:a (2 3)} 4])`
- Whitespace: commas treated as whitespace, leading/trailing whitespace, multiple spaces
- Comments: `; line comment` ignored, comment at end of expression
- String escapes: `\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`, unterminated string → error with position
- Unmatched delimiters: `(1 2`, `[1 2)`, extra `)` → error with position
- Quote shorthand: `'(1 2 3)` → `(quote (1 2 3))`
- Source locations: every AST node carries line and column

**`hob-evaluator-tests`** (~30 tests)

Tests the evaluator and special forms. Uses the reader to construct input ASTs.

- Self-evaluating: numbers, strings, booleans, nil, keywords all return themselves
- Symbol lookup: defined symbol resolves; undefined symbol → error with name and location
- `def`: binds value, returns the value, accessible in subsequent expressions
- `fn`: creates callable, correct arity, closure captures lexical environment
- `let`: basic bindings, sequential (later bindings see earlier ones), body returns last expr
- `if`: true branch, false branch, nil is falsy, `0` and `""` are truthy, two-branch (no else) returns nil
- `do`: evaluates all, returns last, single expression, empty do
- `quote`: returns unevaluated list structure
- `loop`/`recur`: sum 1..10, fibonacci, recur with wrong arity → error
- Function application: simple call, nested calls, higher-order (map), closures (counter factory)
- Async transparency: function returning a Promise → result is auto-awaited, no Promise leaks into Hob land
- Error messages: undefined symbol, wrong arity, calling non-function — all include source location

**`hob-stdlib-tests`** (~35 tests)

Tests built-in standard library functions via `evaluate`. Each test evaluates a Hob expression string and asserts the result.

- Arithmetic: `(+ 1 2)` → 3, `(- 5 3)` → 2, `(* 2 3)` → 6, `(/ 10 2)` → 5, `(mod 7 3)` → 1, `(inc 5)` → 6, `(dec 5)` → 4
- Comparison: `(< 1 2)` → true, `(> 2 1)` → true, `(= 1 1)` → true, `(not= 1 2)` → true
- Strings: `(str "a" "b")` → "ab", `(count "hello")` → 5, `(subs "hello" 1 3)` → "el", `(str/upper-case "hi")` → "HI", `(str/trim " hi ")` → "hi"
- Collections: `(first [1 2 3])` → 1, `(rest [1 2 3])` → [2 3], `(conj [1 2] 3)` → [1 2 3], `(get {:a 1} :a)` → 1, `(assoc {:a 1} :b 2)` → {:a 1 :b 2}, `(count [1 2 3])` → 3, `(empty? [])` → true, `(concat [1] [2 3])` → [1 2 3]
- Nested access: `(get-in {:a {:b 1}} [:a :b])` → 1, `(assoc-in {} [:a :b] 1)` → {:a {:b 1}}
- Higher-order: `(map inc [1 2 3])` → [2 3 4], `(filter (fn [x] (> x 2)) [1 2 3 4])` → [3 4], `(reduce + 0 [1 2 3])` → 6
- Predicates: `(nil? nil)` → true, `(number? 42)` → true, `(string? "hi")` → true, `(vector? [1])` → true, `(map? {:a 1})` → true, `(fn? +)` → true

**`hob-integration-tests`** (~10 tests)

Tests the interpreter's integration with Hobson's storage layer. Requires `testApi` for item operations.

- `get-item` retrieves a previously-created item by ID
- `set-item!` persists an item, retrievable via `api.get()`
- `get-all` returns items (including test fixtures)
- `find-items` with type filter returns matching subset
- Item reference by UUID: `@<uuid>` resolves to the item
- Item reference by name: `@<name>` resolves to the item
- Unresolvable item reference → error with the reference name
- Round-trip: create via `set-item!`, read back via `get-item`, fields match
- Printer output: `(pr-str [1 :a "b"])` produces readable string
- REPL entry point: interpreter's exported `eval` function accepts a string and returns a value

**Phase gate**: Phase 1 is complete when all four test suites pass (target ≥100 tests, 0 failures) and the manual REPL milestone works.

### Phase 2: Macros, Concurrency, and Extended Standard Library

**Goal**: The language becomes extensible and concurrent. Users can define macros. Parallel let and pmap make I/O-heavy code fast by default.

**Deliverables**:
- `defmacro` special form
- Quasiquote / unquote / unquote-splicing in the reader and evaluator
- Macro expansion pass before evaluation
- Destructuring in `let`, `fn`, and `loop`
- Multi-arity functions
- Variadic arguments (`& rest`)
- Standard macros written in Hob: `defn`, `when`, `when-let`, `cond`, `->`, `->>`, `and`, `or`, `for`
- `atom`, `deref`, `swap!`, `reset!`
- `plet` special form and `pmap` built-in
- Auto-parallel `let` optimization (dependency analysis + `Promise.all`)
- `try` / `catch`
- Anonymous function shorthand `#(+ %1 %2)`

**Implementation approach**: Extend the interpreter item. The standard macros are defined in a separate Hob library item (`hob-stdlib`) that the interpreter loads on initialization.

**Estimated size**: ~600 lines added to interpreter JS, ~300 lines of Hob stdlib.

**Testable milestone**:
```lisp
:hob (defmacro unless [test & body]
       `(if ~test nil (do ~@body)))

:hob (-> @my-note
         (. :content)
         (:title)
         (str/upper-case))
;; => "SHOPPING LIST"

;; Concurrency test — these two fetches run in parallel:
:hob (let [notes (find-items {:type @note-type})
           tags  (find-items {:type @tag-type})]
       [(count notes) (count tags)])
```

**`hob-macro-tests`** (~30 tests)

Tests the macro system: `defmacro`, quasiquote reader syntax, macro expansion, and the standard macros written in Hob.

- Quasiquote reader: `` `(a b c) `` reads as `(quasiquote (a b c))`
- Unquote reader: `` `(a ~b c) `` reads with unquote node for `b`
- Unquote-splicing reader: `` `(a ~@xs c) `` reads with splice node for `xs`
- Quasiquote eval: `` (let [x 1] `(a ~x c)) `` => list with `a`, `1`, `c`
- Quasiquote splice eval: `` (let [xs [1 2]] `(a ~@xs c)) `` => list with `a`, `1`, `2`, `c`
- Nested quasiquote: `` `(a `(b ~c)) `` preserves inner quasiquote
- `defmacro` defines a macro callable by name
- Simple macro: `(defmacro my-nil [] nil)` → `(my-nil)` => `nil`
- Macro receives unevaluated forms: `(defmacro quote-first [x & _] `'~x)` → verify `x` is AST, not a value
- `when` macro: `(when true 1 2 3)` => `3`, `(when false 1 2 3)` => `nil`
- `when-let` macro: `(when-let [x 42] (inc x))` => `43`, `(when-let [x nil] (inc x))` => `nil`
- `unless` macro (user-defined): `(defmacro unless [test & body] \`(if ~test nil (do ~@body)))` → `(unless false 42)` => `42`
- `cond` macro: `(cond (= 1 2) "a" (= 1 1) "b" :else "c")` => `"b"`, no matching clause => `nil`
- `defn` macro: `(defn f [x] (+ x 1)) (f 5)` => `6`, sets `_hobName`
- `->` threading: `(-> 1 inc inc)` => `3`, `(-> "hello" str/upper-case (subs 0 3))` => `"HEL"`
- `->>` threading: `(->> [1 2 3] (map inc) (filter (fn [x] (> x 2))))` => `[3 4]`
- `for` macro: `(for [x [1 2 3]] (* x x))` => `[1 4 9]`
- `and` as macro (replaces special form): `(and 1 2 3)` => `3`, `(and 1 nil 3)` => `nil`, short-circuits
- `or` as macro (replaces special form): `(or nil false 3)` => `3`, `(or nil false)` => `false`
- Macro expansion is recursive: macro that expands to another macro call
- Error in macro expansion: body throws → error with macro name and source location
- `gensym` produces unique symbols: `(let [a (gensym "x")] (not= a (gensym "x")))` => `true`
- Anonymous fn shorthand: `#(+ %1 %2)` reads as `(fn [%1 %2] (+ %1 %2))`
- Anonymous fn shorthand eval: `(map #(+ % 10) [1 2 3])` => `[11 12 13]`
- Anonymous fn with `%1 %2 %3`: `(#(+ %1 %2 %3) 1 2 3)` => `6`
- Multi-arity `defn`: `(defn f ([x] (f x 10)) ([x y] (+ x y))) (f 5)` => `15`, `(f 5 20)` => `25`

**`hob-destructuring-tests`** (~25 tests)

Tests destructuring in `let`, `fn`, and `loop` bindings — both sequential (vector) and associative (map) destructuring.

- Vector destructuring in `let`: `(let [[a b c] [1 2 3]] (+ a b c))` => `6`
- Vector destructuring with fewer elements: `(let [[a b] [1 2 3]] b)` => `2` (extra elements ignored)
- Vector destructuring with more elements: `(let [[a b c] [1 2]] c)` => `nil` (missing elements are nil)
- Vector destructuring with rest: `(let [[a & rest] [1 2 3 4]] rest)` => `[2 3 4]`
- Vector destructuring with `_` placeholder: `(let [[_ b _] [1 2 3]] b)` => `2`
- Nested vector destructuring: `(let [[[a b] c] [[1 2] 3]] (+ a b c))` => `6`
- Map destructuring in `let`: `(let [{a :a b :b} {:a 1 :b 2}] (+ a b))` => `3`
- Map `:keys` shorthand: `(let [{:keys [a b]} {:a 1 :b 2}] (+ a b))` => `3`
- Map `:keys` with missing key: `(let [{:keys [a b c]} {:a 1 :b 2}] c)` => `nil`
- Map destructuring with `:or` defaults: `(let [{:keys [a b] :or {b 10}} {:a 1}] (+ a b))` => `11`
- Map destructuring with `:as` whole: `(let [{:keys [a] :as m} {:a 1 :b 2}] (count m))` => `2`
- Mixed nested destructuring: `(let [{:keys [name] [x y] :pos} {:name "p" :pos [10 20]}] [name x y])` => `["p" 10 20]`
- Vector destructuring in `fn` params: `((fn [[a b]] (+ a b)) [1 2])` => `3`
- Map destructuring in `fn` params: `((fn [{:keys [x y]}] (+ x y)) {:x 3 :y 4})` => `7`
- Destructuring in `loop`: `(loop [[x & xs] [1 2 3] acc 0] (if (nil? x) acc (recur xs (+ acc x))))` => `6`
- Map destructuring in `loop`: `(loop [{:keys [n acc]} {:n 5 :acc 1}] (if (<= n 1) acc (recur {:n (dec n) :acc (* acc n)})))` => `120`
- Empty vector destructuring: `(let [[] []] nil)` => `nil` (no error)
- Empty map destructuring: `(let [{} {}] nil)` => `nil` (no error)
- Destructuring a nil value: `(let [[a b] nil] [a b])` => `[nil nil]`
- Map destructuring a nil value: `(let [{:keys [a]} nil] a)` => `nil`
- `defn` with destructured params: `(defn point-str [{:keys [x y]}] (str x "," y)) (point-str {:x 1 :y 2})` => `"1,2"`
- Deeply nested: `(let [{:keys [a] [{:keys [x]}] :items} {:a 1 :items [{:x 42}]}] [a x])` => `[1 42]`
- Error on destructuring non-sequential as vector: `(let [[a] 42] a)` => error mentioning destructuring
- `:strs` shorthand (string keys): `(let [{:strs [name]} {"name" "Alice"}] name)` => `"Alice"`

**`hob-concurrency-tests`** (~20 tests)

Tests auto-parallel `let`, `plet`, and `pmap`. These tests verify correctness and that independent bindings actually run concurrently. The concurrency suite is highest priority — subtle bugs here cause intermittent failures downstream.

- Auto-parallel `let` correctness: `(let [a 1 b 2] (+ a b))` => `3` (basic, both independent)
- Auto-parallel: dependent bindings stay sequential: `(let [a 1 b (+ a 1)] b)` => `2`
- Auto-parallel: mixed independent and dependent: `(let [a 1 b 2 c (+ a b)] c)` => `3` (a,b parallel; c sequential after)
- Auto-parallel timing: two slow operations (using atoms + timeouts to measure) run in less time than sequential
- Auto-parallel: three independent bindings: `(let [a (do (log "a") 1) b (do (log "b") 2) c (do (log "c") 3)] (+ a b c))` => `6`
- Dependency analysis: binding referencing outer scope (not earlier let) is independent: `(def x 1) (let [a x b x] (+ a b))` => `2` (a,b are independent — they reference outer `x`, not each other)
- Dependency analysis: transitive dependency: `(let [a 1 b (+ a 1) c (+ b 1)] c)` => `3` (all sequential)
- `plet` basic: `(plet [a 1 b 2 c 3] (+ a b c))` => `6`
- `plet` all concurrent: verify all bindings in plet run in parallel (timing test with atoms)
- `plet` error: if one binding throws, plet reports the error
- `pmap` basic: `(pmap inc [1 2 3])` => `[2 3 4]`
- `pmap` runs concurrently: verify `pmap` with slow ops completes faster than sequential `map`
- `pmap` with async functions: `(pmap get-item [id1 id2 id3])` returns all items
- `pmap` preserves order: results match input order even when operations complete out of order
- `pmap` empty: `(pmap inc [])` => `[]`
- `pmap` error: if one element throws, `pmap` reports error
- Auto-parallel with item ops: `(let [a (get-item id1) b (get-item id2)] [a b])` — both fetches run concurrently
- `freeSymbols` analysis: verify the dependency analyzer correctly identifies free symbols in nested expressions (internal function test)
- Parallel let with closures: `(let [f (fn [x] x) a (f 1) b (f 2)] [a b])` => `[1 2]` (a,b independent)
- Sequential correctness under parallel: `(let [a (atom 0) b (do (swap! a inc) 1) c (do (swap! a inc) 2)] (deref a))` => `2` (side effects happen regardless of parallelism)

**`hob-atom-tests`** (~15 tests)

Tests mutable reference cells: `atom`, `deref`, `swap!`, `reset!`.

- `atom` creates an atom: `(def a (atom 0)) (type-of a)` => `"atom"`
- `deref` reads value: `(def a (atom 42)) (deref a)` => `42`
- `swap!` applies function: `(def a (atom 0)) (swap! a inc) (deref a)` => `1`
- `swap!` with extra args: `(def a (atom 0)) (swap! a + 5) (deref a)` => `5`
- `swap!` returns new value: `(def a (atom 0)) (swap! a inc)` => `1`
- `reset!` sets value: `(def a (atom 0)) (reset! a 42) (deref a)` => `42`
- `reset!` returns new value: `(def a (atom 0)) (reset! a 42)` => `42`
- Multiple swaps accumulate: `(def a (atom 0)) (swap! a inc) (swap! a inc) (swap! a inc) (deref a)` => `3`
- Atom with complex value: `(def a (atom {:count 0})) (swap! a (fn [m] (assoc m :count (inc (:count m))))) (deref a)` => `{:count 1}`
- Atom in closure: `(def a (atom 0)) (def inc-a (fn [] (swap! a inc))) (inc-a) (inc-a) (deref a)` => `2`

**`hob-error-tests`** (~15 tests)

Tests `try`/`catch` error handling, including nested handlers and rethrowing.

- Basic try/catch: `(try (/ 1 0) (catch e "caught"))` => `"caught"` (or Infinity — test the throw case: `(try (throw "oops") (catch e e))` => `"oops"`)

- Catch division or type error: `(try (+ 1 "a") (catch e "caught"))` => `"caught"`

- Rethrow: inner catch rethrows to outer: `(try (try (throw "err") (catch e (throw (str "re-" e)))) (catch e e))` => `"re-err"`
- `finally` clause (if implemented): `(def a (atom 0)) (try (throw "err") (catch e nil) (finally (swap! a inc))) (deref a)` => `1`

- Error in function call: `(try ((fn [] (throw "fn-err"))) (catch e e))` => `"fn-err"`
- Error message includes source location: `(try (throw "loc-test") (catch e (str e)))` => contains line/col info
- Catch in loop: `(loop [i 0 acc []] (if (>= i 3) acc (recur (inc i) (conj acc (try (if (= i 1) (throw "skip") i) (catch e nil))))))` => `[0 nil 2]`
- Catch preserves environment: `(let [x 42] (try (throw "err") (catch e x)))` => `42`
- Uncaught error propagates: verify that an error not caught by try/catch propagates to the caller

### Phase 3: DOM Runtime and Views

**Goal**: Hob can produce user interfaces. Views written in Hob coexist with JavaScript views.

**Deliverables**:
- Hiccup-to-DOM renderer (mount function)
- Full DOM subtree replacement on re-render (no diffing — keep it simple)
- Event handler wiring (`:on-click`, `:on-change`, etc.)
- CSS class/ID shorthand in keywords (`:div.note-view#main`)
- `def-view` macro that creates view + view-spec items
- Integration with the kernel's rendering system: when a Hob view is found during view lookup, the kernel calls the interpreter to evaluate it
- `render-item` function available in Hob views (delegates to kernel rendering, enables recursive rendering of children)

**Implementation approach**: The DOM runtime is added to the interpreter item. A small adapter layer (could be a separate item or part of kernel-rendering-system) bridges the kernel's view lookup with the Hob interpreter.

The adapter detects whether a view item contains `content.code` (JavaScript) or `content.hob` (Hob source) and routes evaluation accordingly. This means Hob views and JavaScript views coexist transparently.

Diffing is intentionally deferred. Full subtree replacement is correct and simple. If benchmarking reveals performance issues in complex views, integrate morphdom (a lightweight DOM morphing library that can be loaded as a JS library item) as an optimization pass. Building a custom virtual DOM is not planned.

**Estimated size**: ~400 lines JavaScript (DOM runtime, no diffing), ~100 lines Hob (`def-view` macro and helpers).

**Testable milestone**: Create a view in Hob:
```lisp
(def-view @note-type [item]
  [:div.note-view
    [:h2 (or (. item :title) "Untitled")]
    [:p (. item :body)]])
```
Navigate to a note item and see it rendered by the Hob view.

**`hob-dom-tests`** (~30 tests)

Tests the Hiccup→DOM renderer in isolation. Each test constructs a Hiccup vector (Hob data structure), passes it to the DOM runtime's `hiccupToDOM` function, and asserts the resulting DOM tree. Tests run in the browser and have direct `document` access. The renderer is loaded via `api.require('hob-interpreter')` and called as a pure function — no viewport needed.

*Basic elements:*
- `[:div]` → produces a `<div>` element with no children
- `[:p "hello"]` → `<p>` with text content "hello"
- `[:span 42]` → `<span>` with text "42" (numbers auto-stringify)
- `[:div "a" "b" "c"]` → `<div>` with three text nodes
- `[:br]` → self-closing `<br>` element
- `[:input {:type "text" :value "hello"}]` → `<input>` with type and value attributes

*Nested elements:*
- `[:div [:span "inner"]]` → `<div><span>inner</span></div>`
- `[:ul [:li "a"] [:li "b"]]` → `<ul>` with two `<li>` children
- Deep nesting: `[:div [:div [:div [:span "deep"]]]]` — four levels renders correctly
- Mixed children: `[:div "text" [:span "element"] "more text"]` — text and element children interspersed

*CSS class/ID shorthand parsing:*
- `:div.foo` → `<div class="foo">`
- `:div.foo.bar` → `<div class="foo bar">`
- `:div#main` → `<div id="main">`
- `:div#main.foo.bar` → `<div id="main" class="foo bar">`
- `:span.highlight` → `<span class="highlight">`
- `:div` alone → no class or id attributes

*Attribute handling:*
- `[:div {:class "foo"}]` → class attribute set
- `[:div.base {:class "extra"}]` → classes merged: "base extra"
- `[:div {:id "x"}]` → id attribute set
- `[:div {:style {:color "red" :font-size "14px"}}]` → inline style with both CSS properties
- `[:div {:style "color: red"}]` → inline style string set directly
- `[:div {:data-id "123" :title "tooltip"}]` → data attribute and title set
- `[:input {:disabled true}]` → disabled attribute present
- `[:input {:disabled false}]` → disabled attribute absent (not just `disabled="false"`)

*Event handlers:*
- `[:button {:on-click handler} "Click"]` → clicking the button invokes the handler function. Verify with an atom: define `(def clicked (atom false))`, set handler to `(fn [e] (reset! clicked true))`, simulate click, assert `(deref clicked)` is true.
- `[:input {:on-change h1 :on-input h2}]` → multiple events wired independently
- Event handler receives an event argument: handler `(fn [e] (reset! a e))` → `(deref a)` is truthy after click (the DOM event or a Hob-friendly wrapper)

*Children edge cases:*
- `[:div nil "text" nil]` → nil children skipped, `<div>` contains only "text"
- `[:div false "text"]` → false is skipped (consistent with nil)
- `[:div [[:span "a"] [:span "b"]]]` → nested vector flattened: two `<span>` children (supports `for`/`map` output)
- `[:div [] "text"]` → empty vector is a no-op, "text" still renders
- `[:div]` with no children → empty element

*Re-rendering / replacement:*
- Calling `mount` on the same container replaces previous DOM (full subtree replacement)
- Previous event listeners don't fire after replacement (no double-firing — create handler that increments atom, mount, click, remount with different handler, click, check atom value)

### Phase 4: Watches and Events in Hob

**Goal**: The declarative watch system can be authored in Hob. Event-driven code (error handling, lifecycle hooks, search indexing) works in Hob.

**Deliverables**:
- `def-watch` macro that creates library items with `content.watches` and Hob handler code
- Kernel's declarative watch dispatcher supports Hob handlers (calls the interpreter for items with `content.hob`)
- `emit!` function for emitting custom events
- Standard error handler rewritten in Hob as a demonstration

**Implementation approach**: Minimal changes to the kernel's existing `dispatchToWatchers` — just add a branch that detects Hob code and evaluates via the interpreter instead of the JavaScript module system. The kernel already has `callWatchHandler` which loads a module via `moduleSystem.require` and calls a derived handler name (e.g., `onItemUpdated`). The Hob adapter intercepts this: if the watcher item has `content.hob` instead of `content.code`, it evaluates the Hob source and calls the handler function from the Hob environment instead.

**Estimated size**: ~50 lines JavaScript (kernel adapter), ~200 lines Hob (macros + example watches).

**Testable milestone**:

```lisp
  {:event :item/updated :type-extends @note-type}
  [event]
  (log (str "Note updated: " (.. (:item event) :content :title))))
```
Update a note; see the log message.

*def-watch macro structure:*
- `(def-watch "my-watcher" {:event :item/updated} [event api] (log "fired"))` produces a map with `:name`, `:watches`, and `:handler` keys
- The `:watches` value is a vector containing a map with `:event` key
- The `:handler` value is a callable function
- `def-watch` with type filter: `{:event :item/updated :type "11111111-..."}` → watch map contains `:type` in its filter
- `def-watch` with type-extends filter: `{:event :item/updated :type-extends "11111111-..."}` → watch map contains `:typeExtends`
- `def-watch` with id filter: `{:event :item/updated :id "some-guid"}` → watch map contains `:id`
- `def-watch` with multiple filters: `{:event :item/updated :type-extends "..." :id "..."}` → all filters present

*Handler function behavior:*
- Handler receives event content and api as arguments: define handler `(fn [event api] (reset! a (:item event)))`, call it with mock event, verify atom contains the item
- Handler return value is ignored (fire-and-forget semantics)
- Handler that throws does not crash the dispatcher: wrap in try/catch at the caller level, verify other handlers still fire
- Async handler (uses `get-item` or other async ops): handler body with `(get-item "some-id")` completes without error

*emit! function:*
- `(emit! {:type "custom-event-id" :content {:message "hello"}})` creates and dispatches an event — verify by having a watcher that captures it into an atom
- `(emit! {:type event-id :content {:item mock-item}})` with item content — the watcher's filter can match against it
- `emit!` without content: `(emit! {:type event-id})` → event dispatched with nil content, watcher still fires if no filters
- `emit!` returns nil

*Event type matching (hierarchy):*
- Watcher for parent event type receives child events: watch on `ITEM_EVENT` fires for `ITEM_CREATED`, `ITEM_UPDATED`, `ITEM_DELETED`
- Watcher for specific event type only receives that type: watch on `ITEM_UPDATED` does not fire for `ITEM_CREATED`
- Watcher for root `EVENT_DEFINITION` receives all events

*Watch filter evaluation:*
- No filters (only event type) → matches any event of that type
- Type filter → only matches items of exact type
- TypeExtends filter → matches items whose type chain includes the given type
- ID filter → only matches specific item
- Combined filters → all must match (AND semantics)
- Filter with no item in event content → filter fails (returns false), handler not called

*Integration with kernel dispatch:*
- Create a library item with `content.hob` containing a watch handler, add `watches` array to the item, trigger an event → the Hob handler is called (verify via side effect: atom update, item modification, or log capture)
- Hob watcher coexists with JS watcher: both fire for the same event
- Hob watcher with `content.hob` is preferred over `content.code` when both present

*Edge cases:*
- Watcher with multiple watches: first matching watch triggers handler, second does not cause double-fire
- Watcher for unknown event type: no error, handler simply never fires
- Empty watches array: no error, no dispatch

### Phase 5: Reactivity

**Goal**: Views automatically re-render when their dependencies change. No manual watch declarations needed for views.

**Deliverables**:
- Dependency tracker integrated into item access functions
- Tracking context management in the rendering system
- Automatic re-render when tracked items change
- Reactive atoms (dereffing an atom inside a tracking context registers a dependency)
- Integration with the kernel's event system: `item:updated` events trigger dependency-based re-renders for Hob views

**Implementation approach**: The dependency tracker is added to the interpreter item. The rendering adapter (from Phase 3) wraps each Hob view render call in `startTracking()`/`stopTracking()`. The `get-item` built-in is instrumented to call `recordAccess()`.

A new event listener on `item:updated` checks the tracker for affected views and triggers partial re-render (using the existing rendering system's `rerenderItem` capability).

**Estimated size**: ~300 lines JavaScript.

**Testable milestone**: Open a note in the Hob view. Open the REPL and change the note's title:
```lisp

```
The rendered view updates immediately without manual refresh.

**`hob-reactivity-tests`** (~30 tests)

Tests the dependency tracker, tracking contexts, automatic re-render triggering, reactive atoms, and cleanup. Unit tests exercise the tracker directly; integration tests verify the rendering pipeline re-renders on item changes.

*Render Instance Registry:*
- Instances indexed by `itemId`, `viewId`, `parentId`
- `item:updated` → registry lookup → selector check → batched re-render

*Instrumented get-item:*
- `(get-item "some-id")` inside a tracking context records the ID as a dependency
- `(get-item "some-id")` outside a tracking context still works (returns the item, no tracking side effect)
- Multiple `get-item` calls with different IDs in one tracking context records all of them
- Repeated `get-item` with the same ID in one context records it only once (set semantics)

*Reactive atoms:*
- `(deref my-atom)` inside a tracking context registers the atom as a dependency
- `(swap! my-atom inc)` outside tracking does not trigger re-render (no active context)
- `(reset! my-atom value)` triggers re-render of contexts that deref'd the atom
- Atom dependency is distinct from item dependency (atoms are local, items are storage-backed)

*Tracking context lifecycle:*
- Starting a context, calling `get-item`, stopping → returns correct dep set
- Re-render clears old deps before re-tracking (stale deps from previous render removed)
- Context cleanup on unmount removes all subscriptions (no dangling references)
- New deps added on re-render replace old deps (item A was accessed in render 1 but not render 2 → A no longer a dependency)

*Auto re-render on item change:*
- Render a Hob view that calls `(get-item id)`, update that item via `(set-item! ...)`, verify the view re-renders with new data
- Render a view that accesses item A and item B, update only A → view re-renders; update only B → view re-renders
- Render a view that accesses item A, update unrelated item C → view does NOT re-render
- Re-render updates the dependency set: view initially accesses A, after re-render now accesses B instead → updating A no longer triggers re-render, updating B does

*Integration with kernel events:*
- `item:updated` event for a tracked item triggers the re-render callback
- `item:deleted` event for a tracked item triggers the re-render callback (or cleanup)
- `item:created` event does not trigger re-renders (new items can't be in any dep set)
- Multiple views depending on the same item: updating that item re-renders all of them
- Rapid successive updates coalesce into a single re-render (debounce/microtask batching)

*Cycle detection:*
- A re-render that triggers `set-item!` on a tracked item does not cause infinite re-render loop
- Cycle detection breaks the loop after a configurable max depth (e.g., 3 re-renders)
- Warning is logged when a cycle is detected

*Edge cases:*
- View that accesses no items has empty dep set, never auto-re-renders
- View that only uses atoms (no `get-item`) re-renders only on atom changes
- Tracking context with async operations: `get-item` calls inside `plet` or `let` with async values still tracked correctly

## Testing Strategy

All Hob tests use the existing Hobson test infrastructure: `test-lib` for assertions, `test-runner-service` for execution in isolated nested instances, and the `test-suite` type for UI and result persistence. Tests are code items tagged with `test` that export `run(api)`. See the [Testing](item://c0c0c0c0-0036-0000-0000-000000000000) documentation for the full pattern.

**Interpreter exports for testing**: The `hob-interpreter` library exports its internal components (`read`, `evaluate`, `print`, `createEnvironment`) alongside the public `eval` API. This lets test suites exercise the reader and evaluator in isolation without needing the REPL or DOM.

**Test pattern**: Each test evaluates a Hob expression string and asserts the result. Reader tests call `read()` directly and inspect AST nodes. Evaluator and stdlib tests call `evaluate(read(source), env)` and assert return values. Integration tests use `testApi` to create items and verify the interpreter interacts with storage correctly.

**Regression strategy**: When a bug is found, a test is added to the relevant suite before fixing it. The test runner shows all suites in one view, making regressions visible immediately.

**Phase gates**: Each phase defines a minimum test count and zero-failure requirement. A phase is not complete until its tests pass alongside all previous phases' tests.

## Future Direction: Kernel in Hob

The eventual aspiration is a Hobson where the kernel modules are rewritten in Hob, with the interpreter itself being the only JavaScript item. The system would be fully self-describing.

**This is not planned work.** It should only be attempted once Phases 1–6 are stable and battle-tested. It's high-risk because it changes the foundation of the running system.

**Approach**: One module at a time, in order of least to most critical.

**Rewrite order**:

1. **kernel-styles** — Trivially simple, just CSS injection. Good first test.
2. **kernel-safe-mode** — Relatively isolated, only active in safe mode.
3. **kernel-repl** — Moderately complex but well-contained. The Hob REPL replaces the JavaScript REPL.
4. **kernel-viewport** — State management and navigation.
5. **kernel-events** — The event bus. Simple data structure but foundational.
6. **kernel-module-system** — The most conceptually interesting: the module system, written in Hob, loads Hob modules via the interpreter. Requires careful design to avoid circularity.
7. **kernel-rendering-system** — The most complex module. View lookup, instance registry, partial re-render.
8. **kernel-storage** — Validation, type chain checking. Straightforward translation but critical.
9. **kernel-core** — The orchestrator. Depends on all other modules. Rewritten last.

**Bootloader changes**: The bootloader needs a small modification. Currently it loads `kernel-core` as a JavaScript ES module via blob URL. After this, it would load the `hob-interpreter` JavaScript item, then use the interpreter to evaluate `kernel-core` as Hob source.

```javascript
// Current bootloader (simplified)
const kernelModule = await require(KERNEL_CORE_ID);
const Kernel = await kernelModule.loadKernel(require, storageBackend);

// Hob bootloader (simplified)
const interpreterModule = await require(HOB_INTERPRETER_ID);  // JS, loaded as before
const hob = interpreterModule.createInterpreter(storageBackend);
const Kernel = await hob.eval(KERNEL_CORE_ID);  // Hob, evaluated by interpreter
```

**Critical risk**: If the kernel-in-Hob has a bug that prevents boot, the system is bricked. Mitigation: Safe mode must be robust. Consider keeping a JavaScript-based safe mode in the interpreter item that bypasses the Hob kernel entirely. Additionally, the existing export/import mechanism means users always have a JSON backup they can load into a fresh instance.

## Key Design Decisions

### `@` is exclusively for item references

The `@` sigil is reserved for item references (`@my-note`, `@a1b2c3d4-...`). There is no shorthand for atom deref — use `(deref counter)`. This eliminates all ambiguity in the reader: `@` followed by any token is always an item reference. Item references are the more common and more Hobson-native operation, so they get the prime syntax.

### `set-item!` takes no explicit storage argument

The storage backend is implicit in the interpreter's environment. `(set-item! item)` rather than `(set-item! storage item)`. The interpreter knows which store to use. This keeps the language clean — storage is an implementation detail, not a concept Hob users should think about.

### Concurrency via auto-parallel `let` + explicit `plet`/`pmap`

The evaluator automatically parallelizes independent `let` bindings using static AST analysis. `plet` and `pmap` are available for explicit control. This covers the main concurrency need (parallel item fetches) without introducing async concepts into the language. A CPS-based evaluator is the right long-term architecture for UI responsiveness during heavy computation, but is deferred until concrete benchmarks justify the complexity.

### No custom virtual DOM — full replacement now, morphdom later

Phase 3 uses full DOM subtree replacement on re-render. Building a custom virtual DOM diffing algorithm is not planned. If performance becomes an issue, morphdom (a lightweight DOM morphing library) can be integrated as a JS library item. This keeps the initial DOM runtime small (~400 lines) and avoids months of edge-case debugging.

### `for` returns vectors

`for` is a macro that expands to `(vec (map ...))`. It always returns a vector, which is the right type for Hiccup children. No lazy sequences — simplicity over generality.

### JS arrays for vectors, plain objects for maps

Hob vectors are JavaScript arrays. Hob maps are plain JavaScript objects. This makes FFI zero-cost (no marshaling) at the expense of Hob data being mutable from JavaScript. Acceptable tradeoff for a single-user system.

### Phase ordering: watches before reactivity

Watches (Phase 4) are simpler than reactivity (Phase 5) and deliver visible value immediately (logging, indexing, event handling). Reactivity requires careful engineering around dependency tracking and cleanup. Building watches first lets views work with explicit event handling, providing a working baseline before adding automatic reactivity.

---

## Risk Register

### High Risk

**Performance of interpreted evaluation.** Every Hob expression goes through the JavaScript eval loop. If this is too slow for complex views with many children, rendering will feel sluggish. Mitigation: benchmark early (Phase 3), optimize hot paths, and keep JavaScript as an escape hatch for performance-critical code. The DOM runtime in particular should be optimized JavaScript within the interpreter, not interpreted Hob.

**Debugging experience.** When a Hob expression fails, the JavaScript stack trace points into the interpreter's eval function, not at the Hob source. Users need Hob-level stack traces with source locations. This requires the interpreter to maintain a Hob call stack and format it on error. Not hard, but easy to deprioritize and painful if neglected. Must be implemented in Phase 1, not deferred.

### Medium Risk

**Macro hygiene.** Naive macros can accidentally capture variable names from the calling context. This leads to subtle bugs that are hard to diagnose. Mitigation: use gensym (generated unique symbols) in macro definitions, and document the issue clearly. Full hygienic macros (like Scheme's `syntax-rules`) are more complex and probably not worth the implementation cost initially.

**Reactivity cycles.** A view that reads an item, then triggers a write (via an event handler that runs synchronously), could cause an infinite re-render loop. Mitigation: the dependency tracker should detect cycles (same view re-rendering more than N times in a single event cascade) and break with an error.

**FFI boundary complexity.** Passing values between Hob and JavaScript requires marshaling. Using JavaScript arrays for vectors and plain objects for maps makes FFI zero-cost but means Hob data structures are mutable from JavaScript. Accept this tradeoff — Hobson is a single-user system where this is unlikely to cause problems.

### Low Risk

**Language design mistakes.** If a core form turns out to be wrong (e.g., the scoping rules, the destructuring syntax, the item reference semantics), it's painful to change after code has been written. Mitigation: keep the language small. Every feature should be justified by a concrete Hobson use case. Phase 1 and 2 are cheap to iterate on; don't add features speculatively.

**User adoption within Hobson.** Hob is a new language to learn. If the development tools (Phase 6) are weak, users may prefer to keep writing JavaScript. Mitigation: make Phase 6 excellent. The browser, inspector, and query explorer should make Hob feel more productive than JavaScript for Hobson-specific tasks.

---

## Design Principles for Development

These guide decisions throughout implementation.

1. **Each phase delivers standalone value.** No phase depends on future phases for usefulness. Phase 1 is a better REPL. Phase 3 is a better way to write views. Each is worth doing even if you stop there.

2. **JavaScript remains available.** Hob supplements JavaScript; it doesn't replace it by force. Any item can use JavaScript. Migration is gradual and voluntary. There is never a moment where existing JavaScript code stops working.

3. **The interpreter is one item.** The entire Hob implementation is a single library item (plus the Hob stdlib as a second item). It doesn't require special kernel support, additional bootloader complexity, or external dependencies. It's loaded via `require` like any other library.

4. **Hob items are just items.** A Hob code item has `content.hob` instead of (or in addition to) `content.code`. It participates in the same type system, same view lookup, same module system. The kernel's only awareness of Hob is a small adapter that says "if this item has `content.hob`, evaluate it with the interpreter instead of as JavaScript."

5. **Build from within.** Every phase is developed inside the running Hobson system. The interpreter is written in the Hobson REPL. Hob views are tested against live items. The development tools are built using the tools they replace. This is the Smalltalk way.
